1.lecture:
§1 · The quantities, briefly through water, then on their own

Charge (\(Q\), coulombs) is the fundamental quantity; current (\(I\), amperes) is its rate of flow, \(I=dQ/dt\). Voltage (\(V\), volts) is the electrical potential difference that drives current between two points. A water analogy — charge as water volume, current as flow rate, voltage as pressure difference — gives a useful first mental picture, but it breaks down quickly for AC, for reactive elements, and for anything beyond the simplest DC circuit. It is used once, here, and retired; from this point the course works directly with the real electrical quantities.

§2 · Ohm's law and power dissipation

Ohm's law, \(V=IR\), relates voltage across a resistive element to the current through it, with resistance \(R\) (ohms) as the constant of proportionality. Power dissipated in a resistance can be written three equivalent ways — \( P=VI=I^2R=V^2/R \) — pick whichever form uses the two quantities already known. The \(I^2R\) form matters most for a loose or corroded connection: it is simply an extra resistance in series with the rest of the circuit, carrying the same current as everything else on that path. Because power there scales with the square of current, doubling the load current quadruples the heat generated at that one weak point, not merely doubles it — the direct reason a connection that runs only warm at light load can become a genuine fire risk once demand rises, and why torque-checking terminal connections is real maintenance, not a formality.

§3 · Reading a multimeter without folklore

A meter set to measure voltage is connected in parallel across the two points of interest and must present very high input impedance, ideally infinite, so it draws negligible current and does not disturb the circuit or load down a sensitive source. A meter set to measure current is connected in series, breaking the circuit to insert itself, and must present very low input impedance, ideally zero, so it adds no meaningful resistance to the path. The classic, entirely avoidable mistake is leaving a meter in current mode — low internal impedance — and then connecting it in parallel across a voltage source: this creates a near short-circuit through the meter's low internal resistance, drawing a large current that can blow its fuse or damage it outright. Input impedance, matched to the mode setting, is exactly what protects against this in both directions.

§4 · Working method for a basic circuit reading

(1) Identify what is actually being asked — voltage, a difference between two points measured in parallel, or current, a flow through one path measured in series. (2) Set the meter to the matching mode, which sets its internal impedance to match. (3) Apply \(V=IR\), or one of its power forms, using whichever two of \(V\), \(I\), \(R\), \(P\) are already known to find what is missing. This three-step sequence, more than any single formula, is the actual skill this lesson builds.

Key relation — power dissipation

\[ P = I^2R \]

Quadratic in current — the reason a loose connection is genuinely dangerous, not just inefficient.

quiz.q:
Heating at a loose connection

Problem. A loose terminal connection has an effective resistance of \(0.5\ \Omega\). At a normal load current of 10 A it runs warm. If load current doubles to 20 A during peak demand, by what factor does its heating increase, and how much power does it dissipate at each current?
quiz.solution: Solution. \( P=I^2R \): at 10 A, \(P=10^2\times0.5=50\ \text{W}\); at 20 A, \(P=20^2\times0.5=200\ \text{W}\). The factor is \(200/50=4\) — heating quadruples even though current only doubled.

quiz.q:
Direct application of Ohm’s law

Problem. A 12 V battery is connected across a \(4\ \Omega\) resistive load. Find the current and the power dissipated.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( I=V/R=12/4=3\ \text{A} \). \( P=VI=12\times3=36\ \text{W} \) (check: \(P=I^2R=9\times4=36\ \text{W}\), consistent).

quiz.q:
Why the wrong meter mode is dangerous

Problem. Explain what happens, physically, if a multimeter left in its current-measuring mode (low internal impedance) is connected in parallel directly across a 12 V battery terminal, and why this is the actual reason the mode setting matters.
quiz.solution: Solution. The meter's low internal resistance creates a near short-circuit path across the battery; a very large current flows through the meter, limited mainly by the battery's own internal resistance and the meter's small resistance, which can blow the meter's fuse or damage it. The mode setting exists precisely to match the meter's internal impedance to how it needs to be connected — high impedance in parallel for voltage, low impedance in series for current — and using the wrong one for the wrong connection type causes exactly this failure.

quiz.solution: Current is fundamentally a rate.

quiz.q: Voltage is best understood as:
choices: The electrical potential difference driving current between two points
Always equal to power divided by resistance
quiz.solution: Voltage is a difference, always measured between two points.

quiz.q: Ohm's law states:

quiz.solution: Voltage equals current times resistance.

quiz.q: Power dissipated in a resistance can be written as:
choices: P = I²R = V²/R = VI, all equivalent
quiz.solution: All three forms follow directly from Ohm's law; use whichever matches known quantities.

quiz.q: Doubling the current through a fixed resistance changes the dissipated power by a factor of:
quiz.solution: Power is proportional to the square of current.

quiz.q: A voltmeter must be connected:
choices: In parallel, with very high input impedance
In series, with very low input impedance
In parallel, with very low input impedance
In series, with very high input impedance
quiz.solution: High impedance in parallel avoids disturbing the measured circuit.

quiz.q: An ammeter must be connected:
choices: In series, with very low input impedance
In parallel, with very high input impedance
In series, with very high input impedance
In parallel, with very low input impedance
quiz.solution: Low impedance in series avoids adding meaningful resistance to the current path.

quiz.q: Connecting a meter set to current mode in parallel across a voltage source:
choices: Creates a near short-circuit, risking damage or a blown fuse
Is the correct, intended way to measure voltage

quiz.solution: This is the classic, avoidable meter-mode mistake.

1.kuwait:
A thermal imaging survey of a plant switchboard is reading §2 directly off the screen: a terminal glowing noticeably hotter than its neighbors under the same load current is a loose or corroded connection, and because heating there scales with the square of current, a connection that looked merely "warm" on last year's survey at partial load can become genuinely hazardous once the plant runs at full demand — exactly why thermal surveys are scheduled under real operating load, not with equipment idling.

A field technician who leaves a multimeter in current-measuring mode after the last job, then probes it across a live 415 V distribution bus like a voltmeter, is demonstrating §3's warning the expensive way — a blown meter fuse if lucky, a damaged meter and a very loud bang if not. Checking the meter's mode before every measurement, not just before the first one of the shift, is standard field discipline for exactly this reason.

2.lecture:
§1 · Series-parallel reduction

Series resistors carry the same current, and their voltages add: \( R_{series}=R_1+R_2+\dots \) — always larger than the largest individual resistor. Parallel resistors share the same voltage, and their currents add: \( 1/R_{parallel}=1/R_1+1/R_2+\dots \) — always smaller than the smallest individual resistor, because every added parallel branch gives current an additional path, so total resistance to current flow can only fall, never rise, as a branch is added. The working method for any network: redraw it repeatedly, combining series and parallel groups from whichever end is simplest, until a single equivalent resistance remains.

§2 · Kirchhoff's laws

Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL): the sum of currents into any node equals the sum of currents out — charge does not accumulate at a node, so whatever flows in must flow out. Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): the sum of voltage rises and drops around any closed loop equals zero. Physically, KVL conserves energy: starting and returning to the same point means the net potential change must be zero regardless of the path taken around the loop — the same underlying idea that makes a state property path-independent in an entirely different course's energy balances.

§3 · Voltage and current dividers

A voltage divider (series resistors) gives the voltage across any one resistor as the source voltage times that resistor's share of the total series resistance: \( V_{R2}=V_{source}\times\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \). A current divider (two parallel resistors) gives the current through one branch as \( I_{R1}=I_{total}\times\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \) — note the other resistor appears in the numerator, since more current always takes the path of less resistance, so the fraction going through \(R_1\) depends on \(R_2\)'s size, not its own.

§4 · Practical sources and battery-fed control circuits

A real (non-ideal) voltage source carries an internal resistance \(r_{int}\) in series with an ideal source, so its terminal voltage sags under load: \( V_{terminal}=V_{source}-I\,r_{int} \). A battery reads its full open-circuit voltage with nothing connected, but sags noticeably once a real load — a starter motor, a control relay coil — draws current. A weak or aged battery, with a higher \(r_{int}\), can show an entirely acceptable open-circuit voltage yet still fail to crank a real load, because the sag under actual current draw, not the resting voltage, is what determines whether the load actually runs.

Key rule — series and parallel

Series result is always larger than any part. Parallel result is always smaller than any part. Together, this is the entire reduction method.

quiz.q:
Parallel resistance, computed

Problem. Two resistors, \(R_1=6\ \Omega\) and \(R_2=3\ \Omega\), are connected in parallel. Find the equivalent resistance, and explain why it is smaller than either individual resistor.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( 1/R_p=1/6+1/3=1/6+2/6=3/6=1/2\Rightarrow R_p=2\ \Omega \). This is smaller than both 6 Ω and 3 Ω because each parallel branch gives current another path, so the combined resistance to current flow can only decrease as branches are added.

quiz.q:
Voltage divider

Problem. A 12 V source feeds a series combination of \(R_1=8\ \Omega\) and \(R_2=4\ \Omega\). Find the voltage across \(R_2\).
quiz.solution: Solution. \( V_{R2}=12\times\dfrac{4}{8+4}=12\times\dfrac{4}{12}=4\ \text{V} \).

quiz.q:
Verifying KVL around a loop

Problem. A series loop contains a 12 V source, a \(5\ \Omega\) resistor, and a \(7\ \Omega\) resistor, all carrying the same 1 A. Verify Kirchhoff’s Voltage Law around the loop.
quiz.solution: Solution. Drops: \(5\Omega\times1\text{A}=5\text{V}\) and \(7\Omega\times1\text{A}=7\text{V}\), totaling 12 V, exactly matching the 12 V source rise. Net change around the loop: \(12-5-7=0\), confirming KVL — energy conservation requires the loop to net to zero.

choices: Add, giving a result larger than any individual resistor
Combine to a result smaller than any individual resistor
Only add if equal in value
quiz.solution: Series resistance is a simple sum, always larger than any one part.

quiz.q: Resistors in parallel:
choices: Give a result smaller than the smallest individual resistor
Give a result larger than the largest individual resistor
quiz.solution: Every added branch is a new path, so the result can only shrink.

quiz.q: Kirchhoff's Current Law states:
choices: Current into a node equals current out of that node
Voltage around any loop sums to zero
Current only flows into a node, never out
quiz.solution: Charge cannot accumulate at a node.

quiz.q: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law states:
choices: The sum of voltage rises and drops around a closed loop equals zero
Voltage always increases around a loop
Current sums to zero around a loop
Voltage is undefined in a closed loop
quiz.solution: KVL is the loop counterpart to KCL's node balance.

quiz.q: KVL physically conserves:
choices: Energy — potential change around a closed loop must net to zero
Nothing physical; it is a mathematical convention only
quiz.solution: Returning to the same point means net potential change must be zero.

quiz.q: A voltage divider gives the voltage across one series resistor as:
choices: Source voltage times that resistor's share of total series resistance
Source voltage divided by the number of resistors
Independent of the other resistor's value
quiz.solution: V_R2 = V_source × R2/(R1+R2).

quiz.q: A real (non-ideal) voltage source's terminal voltage under load:
choices: Sags below its open-circuit value, due to internal resistance
Stays exactly constant regardless of load
quiz.solution: V_terminal = V_source − I·r_int.

quiz.q: A weak battery can show acceptable open-circuit voltage yet still fail to crank a load because:
choices: Its internal resistance causes significant sag once real current is drawn
Open-circuit voltage always predicts load performance perfectly
Internal resistance has no effect on starting
The battery's chemistry is irrelevant to voltage
quiz.solution: Resting voltage hides a high internal resistance that only shows up under real current draw.

2.kuwait:
A battery-fed control circuit that closes a relay reliably on the bench but fails intermittently in the field is a classic §4 diagnosis: the battery may read a perfectly normal open-circuit voltage, but sag under the relay coil's actual inrush current if its internal resistance has crept up with age — a load test under real current, not a simple voltmeter reading at rest, is what actually confirms a battery is fit for control-circuit duty.

Sizing a shunt resistor to extend an ammeter's range, or a bleeder resistor across a capacitor bank, is a direct current-divider and voltage-divider application respectively — the same §3 formulas a student works on paper are exactly what a control panel designer applies when specifying the resistor values on a schematic.

3.lecture:
§1 · Energy storage in fields

A capacitor stores energy in an electric field between two conductive plates; its current-voltage relation is \( I=C\,dV/dt \) — current depends on how fast voltage is changing, not on the voltage itself, so a capacitor passes no current once fully charged at steady DC (\(dV/dt=0\)). An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field built by current flowing through a coil; its relation is \( V=L\,dI/dt \) — voltage depends on how fast current is changing, so an inductor looks like a plain wire, zero voltage drop, once its current is no longer changing at steady DC.

§2 · First-order transients and time constants

An RC circuit's voltage or current approaches its final value exponentially with time constant \( \tau=RC \); an RL circuit's current does the same with \( \tau=L/R \). After one \(\tau\), a transient is roughly 63% complete; after about five \(\tau\), it is essentially settled. For a contactor coil specifically, \( \tau=L/R \) sets how quickly coil current — and therefore magnetic pull-in force — builds after the coil is energized; a coil with a larger \(\tau\) relative to its resistance takes measurably longer to pull in, a detail that matters for control-timing and for diagnosing a sluggish contactor.

§3 · Why inductive loads arc switches

An inductor resists sudden changes in current: \(V=L\,dI/dt\) means a very fast \(di/dt\) implies, in principle, an unboundedly large voltage. Opening a switch or contact carrying current through an inductive load — a motor coil, a contactor coil, a solenoid — tries to force current from some value to zero almost instantly. The inductor responds by generating a large voltage spike across the opening contact gap, large enough to ionize the air and draw an arc, which continues carrying current — and eroding the contact surface — until the stored magnetic energy is fully dissipated. Suppression — a flyback diode across a DC coil, or an RC snubber across an AC contact — gives that current a controlled alternate path to decay through smoothly instead of being forced to an abrupt stop, keeping \(di/dt\), and the induced voltage, bounded.

§4 · Working method

(1) Identify whether an element stores energy in an electric field (capacitor, current depends on \(dV/dt\)) or a magnetic field (inductor, voltage depends on \(dI/dt\)). (2) For a transient, identify \(R\) and either \(C\) or \(L\) to compute the time constant. (3) For a switching or interruption problem, recognize that any element carrying current through an inductance needs a path for that current to decay smoothly — or expect an arc.

Key relation — the inductive arc

\[ V = L\,\frac{dI}{dt} \]

Forcing di/dt large forces V large — the reason interrupting an inductive circuit arcs, and why suppression works.

quiz.q:
Estimating the interruption voltage spike

Problem. A contactor coil carries 2 A through an inductance of 0.5 H when its contacts open suddenly with no suppression, interrupting the current in about 1 millisecond. Estimate the induced voltage spike.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( V\approx L\,\dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta t}=0.5\times\dfrac{2}{0.001}=1000\ \text{V} \). A spike this size, across a contact gap and coil circuit typically rated for 24-120 V, is easily enough to arc.

quiz.q:
Contactor coil time constant

Problem. A contactor coil circuit has \(L=0.4\ \text{H}\) and coil resistance \(R=8\ \Omega\). Find the time constant, and state roughly how long the coil current takes to reach its final value.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \tau=L/R=0.4/8=0.05\ \text{s}=50\ \text{ms} \). Current reaches roughly 63% of final value after one \(\tau\) (50 ms) and is essentially settled, over 99%, after about five \(\tau\) (250 ms).

quiz.q:
Capacitor current at steady DC

Problem. A capacitor in series with a resistor across a DC source is left connected for many time constants. What current flows through the capacitor at steady state, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. Zero. Once fully charged, \(dV/dt=0\), so \(I=C\,dV/dt=0\) — a fully-charged capacitor passes no current at steady DC, since its current depends only on how fast voltage is still changing, not on the voltage's level.

quiz.q: A capacitor stores energy in:
quiz.solution: Two conductive plates and an insulating gap store energy electrically.

quiz.q: An inductor stores energy in:
quiz.solution: Current flowing through a coil builds a magnetic field that stores the energy.

quiz.q: Current through a capacitor is proportional to:
choices: How fast its voltage is changing, dV/dt
Nothing — capacitor current is always constant
quiz.solution: I = C·dV/dt.

quiz.q: Voltage across an inductor is proportional to:
choices: How fast its current is changing, dI/dt
Nothing — inductor voltage is always zero
quiz.solution: V = L·dI/dt.

quiz.q: At steady DC, a fully-charged capacitor:
quiz.solution: dV/dt = 0 once fully charged, so current is zero.

quiz.q: At steady DC, an inductor with unchanging current:
choices: Has no voltage drop across it
Has maximum voltage drop across it
quiz.solution: dI/dt = 0 once current is steady, so voltage drop is zero.

quiz.q: Interrupting an inductive circuit causes arcing because:
choices: The inductor forces a large voltage spike to oppose the sudden change in current
Current flows backward through the switch
Resistance drops to zero at the contact gap
quiz.solution: A forced fast di/dt implies a forced large V = L·di/dt.

quiz.q: A flyback diode or RC snubber suppresses arcing by:
choices: Giving the inductor's current a controlled path to decay smoothly instead of an abrupt stop
quiz.solution: A smooth decay keeps di/dt, and therefore the induced voltage, bounded.

3.kuwait:
Contactor and relay contacts pitted black and rough well before their rated electrical life is used up are showing exactly the arcing damage of §3 — a missing or failed suppression diode across a DC coil, or a failed snubber across an AC contact, is a common, cheap, and easily overlooked root cause worth checking before simply replacing the contactor and expecting a different result.

A control-timing problem where a contactor "hesitates" noticeably compared to an identical unit elsewhere in the plant often traces back to §2's time constant: a coil circuit with a slightly higher resistance (a corroding connection, again) or a different coil replaced with a mismatched inductance changes L/R enough to be noticeable in a fast sequencing application, even though the contactor "works fine" in isolation.

4.lecture:
§1 · Sinusoids and RMS values

An AC quantity \(v(t)=V_{max}\sin(\omega t)\) constantly changes — its peak value alone overstates its actual heating or working effect, while its average over a full cycle is exactly zero. RMS (root-mean-square) is defined so that an RMS AC value produces the same heating effect — the same average power in a resistor — as an equal DC value. For a pure sinusoid, \( V_{rms}=V_{max}/\sqrt2\approx0.707\,V_{max} \). This is why "230 V AC" is an RMS rating, not a peak — the honest, working-equivalent number, with the actual peak running about \(\sqrt2\) times higher, near 325 V.

§2 · Phasor representation

A phasor is a rotating-vector shorthand for a sinusoid at one fixed frequency: instead of tracking \(v(t)\) through time, a phasor captures a sinusoid's magnitude and phase angle as a single vector or complex number. This turns the calculus of sinusoids — derivatives and integrals — into simple vector or complex algebra, a simplification that works because every quantity in a linear AC circuit at one frequency is itself a sinusoid at that same frequency, differing only in magnitude and phase.

§3 · Impedance of R, L, C

Impedance \(Z\) generalizes resistance to AC. A resistor's impedance is simply \(Z_R=R\), no phase shift. An inductor's impedance, \(Z_L=j\omega L\), grows with frequency and shifts voltage \(90^\circ\) ahead of current. A capacitor's impedance, \(Z_C=1/(j\omega C)\), shrinks with frequency and shifts the opposite way — current ahead of voltage. Impedance adds to resistance exactly the concept resistance alone in a DC circuit never needed: a phase relationship between voltage and current, not just a magnitude ratio. Series impedances combine the same way resistances do, \(Z_{total}=Z_1+Z_2+\dots\), but as complex numbers, so magnitudes generally do not simply add unless the phase angles happen to match.

§4 · The 50 Hz world, quantified

At \(f=50\ \text{Hz}\) (or 60 Hz in some countries), \( \omega=2\pi f\approx314\ \text{rad/s} \) — this single number turns \(L\) (henries) and \(C\) (farads) into actual ohms of impedance at mains frequency: \(Z_L=\omega L\), \(Z_C=1/(\omega C)\). Working method: (1) convert every element to its impedance at the operating frequency; (2) combine impedances exactly as resistances combine — series adds, parallel via reciprocals — but with complex arithmetic; (3) apply the AC form of Ohm's law, \(V=IZ\), using RMS magnitudes and phase angles as needed.

Key rating — RMS

\[ V_{rms} = \frac{V_{max}}{\sqrt2} \]

The honest, heating-equivalent rating for a sinusoid — not the peak.

quiz.q:
Peak to RMS

Problem. A sinusoidal voltage has a peak value of 325 V. Find its RMS value, and explain briefly why RMS, not peak, is the honest rating for this quantity.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( V_{rms}=325/\sqrt2\approx230\ \text{V} \). RMS is the honest rating because it is defined to give the same heating/working effect as an equal-valued DC source; quoting the 325 V peak would overstate the actual effect of the voltage, since the sinusoid spends most of the cycle below that peak.

quiz.q:
Impedance from R and X

Problem. A series RL circuit has \(R=3\ \Omega\) and inductive reactance \(X_L=4\ \Omega\) at the operating frequency. Find the impedance magnitude.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( Z=\sqrt{R^2+X_L^2}=\sqrt{9+16}=\sqrt{25}=5\ \Omega \).

quiz.q:
Reactance vs. frequency

Problem. An inductor has \(L=0.1\ \text{H}\). Find its reactance at 50 Hz, and state whether reactance rises or falls as frequency increases.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( X_L=\omega L=2\pi(50)(0.1)\approx31.4\ \Omega \). Inductive reactance rises with frequency — the opposite of a capacitor, whose reactance falls as frequency increases.

quiz.q: RMS voltage for a sinusoid relates to peak voltage by:
quiz.solution: The √2 factor comes from averaging the square of a sine wave over a full cycle.

quiz.q: RMS is the standard AC rating because:
choices: It gives the same heating/working effect as an equal DC value
It is always the largest possible value
It is easier to measure than peak voltage
It is defined arbitrarily by convention with no physical basis
quiz.solution: The equal-heating definition is what makes RMS physically meaningful.

quiz.q: A phasor represents a sinusoid's:
choices: Magnitude and phase angle as a single vector/complex number
Instantaneous value at every point in time
quiz.solution: Phasors work because magnitude and phase fully describe a fixed-frequency sinusoid.

quiz.q: A resistor's impedance:
choices: Equals its resistance, with no phase shift
quiz.solution: Resistance is frequency-independent and introduces no phase shift.

quiz.q: An inductor's impedance:
choices: Grows with frequency and shifts voltage ahead of current by 90°
Is identical to a resistor's impedance
quiz.solution: Z_L = ωL — directly proportional to frequency.

quiz.q: A capacitor's impedance:
choices: Is identical to an inductor's impedance
quiz.solution: Z_C = 1/(ωC) — inversely proportional to frequency.

quiz.q: Impedance adds to resistance the concept of:
choices: A phase relationship between voltage and current
A new unit of measurement unrelated to ohms
quiz.solution: Phase, not just magnitude, is what impedance adds beyond plain resistance.

quiz.q: Series impedances combine by:
choices: Subtracting the smaller from the larger
quiz.solution: The same addition rule as series resistance, generalized to complex impedance.

4.kuwait:
Nameplate voltage and current ratings on virtually every piece of plant electrical equipment are RMS values by convention — a fact worth remembering when a technician sees a scope trace peaking well above the nameplate number and mistakes an entirely normal sinusoid for an overvoltage event, when the RMS reading on a properly-set meter would show the equipment operating exactly within its rated envelope.

A cable or contactor sized only by its resistive (DC-style) current rating, without accounting for the reactive impedance of a long run or an inductive load, can be under-specified in a real AC installation — the working method of §4, converting every element to its actual impedance at 50 Hz before sizing anything, is what a competent electrical design review checks for on a new installation drawing.

5.lecture:
§1 · Real, reactive, and apparent power

Real power \(P\) (watts, kW) is what is actually converted to useful work or heat — a motor's shaft output, a heater's element. Reactive power \(Q\) (VAR, kVAR) oscillates back and forth between the source and any inductive or capacitive element, doing no net useful work but still requiring current to flow to sustain the magnetic or electric field involved. Apparent power \(S\) (VA, kVA) is simply \(S=VI\) using RMS values — what the supply, the wiring, the transformer, the generator, must actually be sized to carry, since it must carry the current whether or not that current does useful work. The three relate as a right triangle, \(S^2=P^2+Q^2\), with the angle \(\varphi\) between \(P\) and \(S\) equal to the angle by which current lags or leads voltage.

§2 · Power factor — why utilities bill for it

Power factor, \( PF=P/S=\cos\varphi \), is the fraction of apparent power actually doing useful work. A plant at \(PF=0.7\) draws \(S=P/0.7\approx1.43P\) in apparent power for the same real power — the wiring, transformers, and generation capacity serving that plant must all be sized roughly 43% larger than the real power alone would require, purely to carry reactive current that does no useful work. Utilities bill for poor power factor, directly through a penalty or indirectly through a demand/kVA charge, because that extra capacity is real infrastructure cost they bear whether or not it ever converts to useful output for the customer.

§3 · Correction capacitors, sized

Most industrial reactive power is inductive — motors, transformers, ballasts — a lagging power factor. A capacitor bank connected locally supplies leading reactive power that cancels some or all of the inductive reactive power the load demands from the utility; the load still draws its full original reactive current, but in a short local loop with the capacitor rather than all the way back through the utility's own wiring and transformers. The reactive power the utility actually sees becomes \( Q_{new}=Q_{old}-Q_{capacitor} \); sizing a correction bank means computing the reactive-power reduction needed to reach a target power factor and selecting a capacitor rated for that many kVAR.

§4 · Working method

(1) Find \(P\), the real power, from the load's actual work output or a direct measurement. (2) Find \(Q\) or \(S\) from measurement, or from the load's known power factor. (3) Apply \(S^2=P^2+Q^2\) for whichever of the three is still missing. (4) If correction is needed, compute the target \(Q\) at the desired power factor, and the capacitor kVAR required to close the gap between the actual \(Q\) and that target.

Key relations — power triangle

\[ S^2=P^2+Q^2,\qquad PF=\frac{P}{S}=\cos\varphi. \]

quiz.q:
Apparent and reactive power at PF=0.7

Problem. A plant draws 800 kW of real power at a power factor of 0.7. Find the apparent power the utility must supply, and the reactive power flowing that does no useful work.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( S=P/PF=800/0.7\approx1143\ \text{kVA} \). \( Q=\sqrt{S^2-P^2}=\sqrt{1143^2-800^2}\approx816\ \text{kVAR} \).

quiz.q:
Power triangle, classic values

Problem. A load draws real power \(P=800\ \text{kW}\) and reactive power \(Q=600\ \text{kVAR}\). Find the apparent power and the power factor.

Sizing a correction capacitor

Problem. A plant’s power factor is to be corrected from 0.7 to 0.95 at a constant real power of 800 kW. Find the reactive power before and after correction, and the capacitor kVAR needed.
quiz.solution: Solution. Before: \( \varphi_1=\arccos(0.7)\approx45.6^\circ \), \( Q_1=P\tan\varphi_1\approx800\times1.020\approx816\ \text{kVAR} \) (matches the apparent-power result above). After: \( \varphi_2=\arccos(0.95)\approx18.2^\circ \), \( Q_2=800\times0.329\approx263\ \text{kVAR} \). Capacitor needed: \( Q_1-Q_2\approx816-263\approx553\ \text{kVAR} \).

quiz.solution: P is the useful component, measured in watts.

quiz.q: Reactive power (Q) is:
choices: Power that oscillates without doing net useful work, but still requires current
quiz.solution: Q sustains fields in inductors/capacitors without net useful output.

quiz.q: Apparent power (S) is:
choices: What the supply (wiring, transformer) must be sized to carry
quiz.solution: S=VI sets the actual current the supply infrastructure must handle.

quiz.q: Power factor is defined as:
quiz.solution: PF is the useful fraction of apparent power.

quiz.q: A plant with a low power factor:
choices: Draws more apparent power (and current) than its real power alone would require
Draws less apparent power than its real power
Has no effect on supply sizing
quiz.solution: Low PF means a larger S is needed for the same useful P.

quiz.q: Utilities bill for poor power factor because:
choices: The extra apparent power represents real infrastructure capacity they must provide
It is a purely arbitrary penalty with no cost basis
It only affects the customer's own equipment
quiz.solution: Wiring and transformers must be sized for the full apparent power, low-PF or not.

quiz.q: Correction capacitors improve power factor by:
choices: Supplying local leading reactive power that cancels inductive reactive power
Removing all current from the circuit
quiz.solution: Leading Q from the capacitor offsets lagging Q from inductive loads.

quiz.q: After adding correction capacitors, the reactive power the UTILITY sees:
choices: Decreases, even though the motor's own reactive current locally is unchanged
quiz.solution: The capacitor supplies the reactive current locally, sparing the utility's own wiring from carrying it.

5.kuwait:
A plant electricity bill carrying a power-factor penalty line item is the utility charging directly for §2's extra apparent-power capacity — a maintenance or energy team that installs correction capacitor banks sized by §3's method routinely sees that penalty disappear within a billing cycle, often the single fastest-payback electrical project available on an older, heavily-motor-loaded site.

A transformer or feeder that trips on overcurrent well before its nameplate kVA would suggest, at a load that "shouldn't" be that large, is frequently a low-power-factor problem in disguise: the real power looks modest, but the actual apparent power — and the current the feeder must physically carry — is inflated by uncorrected reactive power exactly as §1 describes.

6.lecture:
§1 · Wye and delta connections

A wye (star) connection joins three windings or loads at a common neutral point, with each winding's other end brought out as a line — this gives access to both line-to-line and line-to-neutral voltages. A delta connection joins the three windings end-to-end in a closed loop, with each junction brought out as a line — there is no neutral point, and only line-to-line voltage exists. Line quantities, measured on the supply conductors, and phase quantities, measured across one winding or load element, are not automatically the same — the relationship between them depends entirely on which connection is used.

§2 · Line vs. phase relationships

In a wye connection: \( V_{line}=\sqrt3\,V_{phase} \) — line voltage is about 1.732 times the phase voltage — while \( I_{line}=I_{phase} \), since line and winding share the same series path. The \(\sqrt3\) factor comes directly from the \(120^\circ\) phasor geometry: the vector difference between two phase voltages \(120^\circ\) apart has magnitude \(\sqrt3\) times either one's magnitude, not simply double. In a delta connection, it reverses: \( V_{line}=V_{phase} \), since each line connects directly across one winding, while \( I_{line}=\sqrt3\,I_{phase} \), since each line current is now the phasor difference of two phase currents.

§3 · Three-phase power — constant delivery

Three-phase real power is given compactly by \( P=\sqrt3\,V_{line}I_{line}\,PF \), using line quantities directly regardless of whether the system is wye or delta — exactly why this is the practical field formula. A single-phase voltage and current both cross zero twice per cycle, so their instantaneous product — power — also dips to zero twice per cycle: single-phase power delivery pulses. With three phases spaced \(120^\circ\) apart, each phase's instantaneous power still pulses individually, but the three pulses stagger exactly enough that their sum is mathematically constant for a balanced load — the real reason three-phase motors run smoother than single-phase ones, with no torque pulsation traceable to the electrical supply itself.

§4 · Why industrial plants are three-phase places

Three-phase delivers more power per conductor weight than an equivalent single-phase system, provides constant, non-pulsating power delivery to motors — smoother torque, less vibration, less mechanical stress — and is what allows a simple, cheap rotating magnetic field, the subject of Lesson 8, to be generated directly from the supply with no extra starting circuitry. Working method: (1) determine whether the connection is wye or delta; (2) convert between line and phase quantities using \(\sqrt3\) in the correct direction for that connection; (3) apply \(P=\sqrt3\,V_{line}I_{line}\,PF\) for total three-phase real power.

Key ratios — wye vs. delta

Wye: \( V_{line}=\sqrt3\,V_{phase} \), \( I_{line}=I_{phase} \). Delta: \( V_{line}=V_{phase} \), \( I_{line}=\sqrt3\,I_{phase} \).

Wye line voltage

Problem. A wye-connected three-phase supply has a phase voltage of 230 V. Find the line voltage, and state which ratio connects the two.

quiz.q:
Three-phase power delivered

Problem. A three-phase motor draws a line current of 20 A from a 400 V line-to-line supply at power factor 0.85. Find the real power delivered.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( P=\sqrt3\,V_{line}I_{line}\,PF=1.732\times400\times20\times0.85\approx11{,}778\ \text{W}\approx11.8\ \text{kW} \).

Delta line current

Problem. A delta-connected load has a phase (winding) current of 15 A. Find the line current.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( I_{line}=\sqrt3\,I_{phase}=1.732\times15\approx26.0\ \text{A} \).

quiz.solution: Delta has no common junction to bring out as a neutral.

quiz.solution: Vector subtraction of two 120°-apart phasors produces exactly this factor.

choices: P = 3 × V_phase × I_phase × PF always, regardless of connection type shown in line form
quiz.solution: This line-quantity formula works regardless of wye or delta connection.

quiz.q: Single-phase instantaneous power:
choices: Pulses, dropping to zero twice per cycle
quiz.solution: Voltage and current both cross zero twice per cycle, so their product does too.

quiz.q: Balanced three-phase instantaneous power delivery is:
choices: Constant, because the three phases' pulses are staggered to sum evenly

quiz.solution: The 120° stagger between phases is what makes the sum constant.

quiz.q: A key practical advantage of three-phase for motors is:
choices: Constant, non-pulsating torque delivery from the supply itself
No need for any winding at all
quiz.solution: Smooth torque, not merely smooth voltage, is the practical payoff.

6.kuwait:
A plant one-line diagram showing "400/230 V" on the main distribution transformer is stating a wye secondary directly: 230 V is the phase (line-to-neutral) voltage feeding single-phase loads like lighting and small equipment, while 400 V is the line-to-line voltage feeding three-phase motors and larger loads, both delivered from the exact same winding by the √3 relationship of §2 — a detail worth understanding before assuming a "400 V" and a "230 V" circuit come from two different sources.

Balanced loading across the three phases is a standing electrical-maintenance concern precisely because §3's constant-power result only holds when the load actually is balanced; a plant that has grown single-phase loads unevenly across its three phases over the years loses some of three-phase's smooth-delivery advantage and can show measurable current imbalance between phases on a clamp-meter reading, worth correcting before it shows up as uneven heating in the supply transformer.

7.lecture:
§1 · Ideal transformer relations

A transformer transfers energy between two circuits through a shared magnetic core, with no direct electrical connection between primary and secondary windings. The turns ratio \(a=N_1/N_2\) sets the voltage transformation directly: \(V_1/V_2=N_1/N_2=a\). Current transforms the opposite way: \(I_1/I_2=N_2/N_1=1/a\) — more secondary turns means lower secondary current for the same power, and vice versa. What stays nearly constant is apparent power: \(S_1\approx S_2\) for an ideal device, since turns change the voltage-current split, not the total VA transferred.

§2 · Real losses

Real transformers carry two loss types. Core losses — hysteresis and eddy-current losses in the iron — are roughly constant whenever the transformer is energized, independent of load. Copper losses — \(I^2R\) losses in the winding resistance — grow with the square of load current, and are zero at no load. Because one loss is roughly load-independent and the other grows with the square of load, peak efficiency occurs somewhere between no-load and full-load, where the two loss types are roughly balanced — not necessarily at full rated load, a common misreading of a nameplate.

§3 · Nameplate data decoded

Two numbers matter first. The kVA rating is the transformer's apparent-power capacity — it sets the maximum current the unit can safely carry at rated voltage, tying directly to Lesson 5's apparent power. The voltage ratio (e.g. "11000/400 V") states what it is built to step between. Beyond those two: impedance percentage (\(\%Z\)) sets both voltage regulation under load and, critically, the fault current magnitude available if the secondary is bolted-shorted — a low \(\%Z\) transformer can deliver very high fault current, a real protection-coordination consideration. Vector group states the phase relationship between primary and secondary, which matters when paralleling transformers or matching downstream equipment.

§4 · Distribution transformers in a plant one-line diagram

A one-line diagram shows a transformer as a simple symbol carrying its kVA and voltage-ratio label. Reading it: (1) identify the voltage transformation — what feeds in, what comes out; (2) check the kVA rating against the actual connected or expected load for real capacity headroom; (3) note \(\%Z\) for protective-device coordination downstream. This reading skill, not detailed transformer design, is what a mechanical engineer working around plant power systems actually needs.

Key relations — the ideal transformer

\[ \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{N_1}{N_2},\qquad \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{N_2}{N_1},\qquad S_1\approx S_2. \]

7.kuwait:
A plant one-line diagram review, before any new equipment is added downstream of an existing distribution transformer, is exactly §4's working method in practice: checking the transformer's kVA rating against the new total connected load, not just against what it was originally specified for years earlier, is what catches an overloaded transformer before it trips or, worse, overheats in service.

A transformer running noticeably warmer than its neighbors of similar rating and load, confirmed by a thermal survey, is often flagged for a %Z and loss check rather than an immediate replacement — an aging unit with rising core losses (from insulation or lamination degradation) can run hot well before its winding actually fails, and catching that trend early is cheaper than an unplanned outage later.

8.lecture:
§1 · The rotating field and slip

Three-phase stator windings, fed by currents \(120^\circ\) apart in time and arranged \(120^\circ\) apart in space (Lesson 6's phasor geometry), produce a magnetic field that physically rotates at synchronous speed \( N_s=120f/p \) (\(f\)=supply frequency, \(p\)=number of poles) — no moving parts required to create the rotation itself. The rotor is dragged along by induction — hence the name — but can never actually reach synchronous speed: doing so would eliminate relative motion between rotor and field, eliminating induced rotor current and therefore torque. Slip, \( s=(N_s-N)/N_s \), is always strictly greater than zero for a loaded induction motor.

§2 · The torque-speed curve — reading it

Starting torque, at \(s=1\) (standstill), is moderate. As the rotor speeds up, torque rises to a peak — the breakdown (or pullout) torque — at some intermediate slip, then falls steeply as slip continues to fall toward zero. The motor's normal rated operating point sits at small slip, often \(2\)–\(5\)%, well past the breakdown-torque peak, on the steep falling section of the curve — exactly where a small speed change produces a proportionally larger torque change, giving the machine its natural, stable, self-correcting load-following behavior. If load torque exceeds breakdown torque, the motor stalls outright — a hard physical limit, not a gradual slowdown.

§3 · Nameplate literacy

Power (kW or hp) is the rated mechanical shaft output, not electrical input, which runs somewhat higher by the efficiency. Current is the rated full-load current at rated voltage, frequency, and load — the number a thermal overload is sized against. Service factor (e.g. 1.15) is a margin above nameplate power the motor can sustain continuously without exceeding its insulation temperature limits, under the rated conditions — not a routine overload allowance, since sustained operation there runs the insulation closer to its ceiling and shortens expected life compared to running at the 100% nameplate rating. Insulation class (B, F, H) states the maximum continuous winding temperature the insulation is built to withstand — the real thermal ceiling that service factor and ambient-temperature derating both have to respect.

§4 · Working method — the machine that drives nearly everything

(1) Read or compute slip and synchronous speed from \( N_s=120f/p \) and the nameplate's rated speed. (2) Locate the machine's normal operating point on the torque-speed curve — small slip, steep region, well below breakdown torque, for margin against load spikes. (3) Read power, current, service factor, and insulation class together as one coherent thermal and mechanical rating, not four unrelated numbers.

Key definition — slip

\[ s=\frac{N_s-N}{N_s} > 0\quad\text{always, for a loaded induction motor.} \]

quiz.q:
Synchronous speed and slip

Problem. A 4-pole induction motor runs on a 50 Hz supply at a measured 1455 rpm under load. Find the synchronous speed and the slip, and explain why the motor cannot run at exactly synchronous speed.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( N_s=120\times50/4=1500\ \text{rpm} \). \( s=(1500-1455)/1500=0.03 \) (3%). It cannot reach synchronous speed because doing so would eliminate relative motion between rotor and field, eliminating induced rotor current and torque — with no torque, it could not overcome friction or load, so it always settles at a slightly lower speed under any real load.

quiz.q:
Reading a service factor

Problem. A motor nameplate reads "15 kW, service factor 1.15." What continuous power output does this actually permit, and is it an invitation to routinely run the motor there?
quiz.solution: Solution. SF 1.15 permits continuous operation up to \(15\times1.15=17.25\ \text{kW}\) under the rated conditions. It is not a routine overload allowance — sustained operation at the service-factor limit runs the insulation closer to its thermal ceiling and shortens expected life compared to the 15 kW nameplate rating; it exists as an occasional margin, not a new normal operating point.

quiz.q:
Exceeding breakdown torque

Problem. A pump load’s required torque rises above an induction motor’s breakdown torque during a startup transient (a partially closed valve). What happens to the motor?
quiz.solution: Solution. The motor stalls — it cannot accelerate past the point where load torque exceeds available motor torque. Held there, it draws high stalled current similar to locked-rotor current while producing little useful shaft output, risking a thermal trip or winding damage if not cleared quickly.

quiz.q: Slip is defined as:
quiz.solution: Slip is the fractional speed deficit relative to synchronous speed.

quiz.q: On the torque-speed curve, breakdown (pullout) torque is:
choices: The peak torque the motor can produce before stalling

quiz.solution: Exceeding this peak causes the motor to stall outright.

quiz.q: A motor's normal rated operating point sits:
choices: At small slip, on the steep part of the curve past breakdown torque
quiz.solution: Small slip, steep region — stable and load-following.

quiz.q: Nameplate power rating refers to:
quiz.solution: Electrical input is somewhat higher, by the motor's efficiency.

quiz.q: Service factor 1.15 permits:

quiz.solution: It is a rated margin, not routine practice.

quiz.q: Insulation class defines:
choices: The maximum continuous winding temperature the motor is rated to withstand
quiz.solution: Insulation class sets the real thermal ceiling everything else must respect.

8.kuwait:
A pump or fan motor that "hums and does not start" under load, rather than tripping instantly, is very often stalled past its breakdown torque — a partially closed downstream valve, a jammed impeller, or a badly misaligned coupling can all raise the required starting torque past what the motor can deliver, and recognizing this from the torque-speed curve, rather than assuming an electrical fault first, often finds the real mechanical cause faster.

A maintenance program that logs measured running current against nameplate full-load current at regular intervals is tracking slip and loading indirectly: a motor drawing noticeably more current than its historical baseline at the same process condition is carrying more load — a bearing dragging, a misalignment, a partially blocked flow path — well before that trend shows up as an outright trip or failure.

9.lecture:
§1 · The synchronous machine, from the other side

A synchronous machine's rotor carries a magnetic field — usually an electromagnet fed by DC excitation current through slip rings or a brushless exciter. Unlike an induction motor's rotor, dragged along behind the field by induced current, a synchronous machine's rotor field locks directly onto the stator's rotating field and turns with it, at exactly synchronous speed, with no slip at all. As a motor, it locks its rotor to the rotating stator field and turns at fixed synchronous speed regardless of load, up to its torque limit — beyond that limit it simply loses synchronism and stalls, rather than slowing gradually the way an induction motor does. As a generator (alternator), the same machine is spun by an external prime mover — a diesel engine, a steam or gas turbine — at synchronous speed, and its rotating DC-excited field induces AC voltage in the stationary stator windings. This is how virtually all utility and standby generation actually works.

§2 · Standby diesel generator sets

A genset's kW/kVA rating reflects both the engine's mechanical power and the alternator's electrical capacity, sized to the actual critical load plus margin for motor-starting inrush — a large motor's starting current can run 5-7 times its running current, and the genset must supply that surge without its own voltage or frequency collapsing. Load acceptance describes how much load the set can accept in one step without stalling the engine or causing an unacceptable voltage or frequency dip — a genset sized only on steady-state kW can still fail in practice if it cannot accept the actual step-load a real outage throws at it. The transfer sequence: an automatic transfer switch detects utility failure, starts the genset, waits for stable voltage and frequency, then transfers the critical load — and reverses the process automatically once utility power returns and proves stable.

§3 · Paralleling conditions

Before closing a breaker to connect a generator onto a live bus — paralleling with the utility, or with other generators — three quantities must match: voltage magnitude, frequency, and phase angle. Closing the breaker with any of these mismatched drives a large, potentially damaging circulating current between the generator and the bus, from the instantaneous voltage difference across the closing contacts — severe enough to damage the generator, the bus, or both. Automatic synchronizing relays exist specifically to check and hold off closure until all three genuinely match, rather than trusting a manual judgment call.

§4 · Working method

(1) Identify whether the machine is acting as a motor, locked to the supply at fixed speed, or a generator, driven by a prime mover and creating the supply. (2) For standby power, size the genset against both steady-state load and the worst-case step-load or motor-starting inrush, never steady-state alone. (3) Before any paralleling operation, verify voltage, frequency, and phase match through the actual synchronizing equipment — never assume.

Key rule — paralleling

Voltage, frequency, and phase angle must all match before the breaker closes — mismatched closure drives damaging circulating current.

quiz.q:
Poles for a fixed genset speed

Problem. A standby generator set is driven at a fixed 1500 rpm by its diesel engine. For a 50 Hz output, how many poles must the alternator have?
quiz.solution: Solution. \( p=120f/N_s=120\times50/1500=4 \) poles.

quiz.q:
The three paralleling conditions

Problem. List the three quantities that must match before a standby generator’s breaker can close to parallel it onto a live bus, and explain briefly what happens if they do not.
quiz.solution: Solution. Voltage magnitude, frequency, and phase angle. If any is mismatched at closure, a large circulating current flows between generator and bus, driven by the instantaneous voltage difference — potentially damaging the generator windings, the bus, or the breaker itself. Synchronizing equipment exists specifically to verify all three before allowing closure.

quiz.q:
Sizing for motor-starting surge

Problem. A standby genset supplies a critical load of 200 kW steady-state, of which the largest motor draws 40 kW running with a starting current 6 times its running current. Roughly how much surge kW must the genset accept momentarily when that motor starts, on top of the rest of the steady load?
quiz.solution: Solution. Approximating kW inrush scaling with current inrush, the starting motor alone demands roughly \(40\times6=240\ \text{kW}\) momentarily. With the remaining \(200-40=160\ \text{kW}\) of the load already running steadily, the genset must briefly accept on the order of \(160+240=400\ \text{kW}\) — well above its 200 kW steady-state rating — which is exactly why motor-starting inrush, not steady load, is often the real genset sizing constraint.

quiz.q: A synchronous machine's rotor:
choices: Locks to the stator's rotating field and turns at exactly synchronous speed
Is dragged along behind the field, like an induction motor
Has no magnetic field of its own
Turns at a speed independent of the supply frequency
quiz.solution: Locking, not slip, is what defines synchronous operation.

quiz.q: Unlike an induction motor, a synchronous machine:
choices: Cannot run at any speed other than synchronous speed under normal operation
Always has more slip than an induction motor
Cannot be used as a generator
quiz.solution: It runs at exactly Ns or loses synchronism (stalls) — no intermediate state.

quiz.q: As a generator, a synchronous machine is:
choices: Driven by an external prime mover at synchronous speed to induce AC voltage
Fed AC power to create rotation
Identical in operation to an induction generator
quiz.solution: Mechanical input at synchronous speed produces the electrical output.

quiz.q: A genset's load acceptance rating describes:
choices: How much load it can accept in one step without stalling or excessive voltage/frequency dip
quiz.solution: Step-load capability, not just steady-state capacity, is the real sizing question.

quiz.q: An automatic transfer switch (ATS):
choices: Detects utility failure, starts the genset, and transfers load once stable
Has no role in genset systems
quiz.solution: ATS handles the full failure-detection-to-transfer sequence automatically.

quiz.q: Before paralleling a generator onto a live bus, the three quantities that must match are:
quiz.solution: These three, verified by synchronizing equipment, before the breaker closes.

quiz.q: Closing a breaker with mismatched voltage/frequency/phase causes:
choices: A large, potentially damaging circulating current
A permanent increase in generator efficiency
quiz.solution: The instantaneous voltage difference across the closing contacts drives this current.

quiz.q: Sizing a standby genset requires considering:
choices: Both steady-state load and worst-case motor-starting inrush
Only the total connected nameplate horsepower
Only the smallest motor on the load
quiz.solution: Starting inrush is often the more demanding, easily overlooked sizing case.

9.kuwait:
A standby generator that starts and runs fine on a load bank test, but stalls or trips the moment the actual critical pump motor starts during a real outage, is almost always a §2 load-acceptance and starting-inrush sizing gap — the steady-state kW number on the genset nameplate looked adequate, but nobody checked whether it could absorb the largest motor's actual starting surge on top of everything else already running.

A generator paralleling panel with automatic synchronizing relays is not a convenience feature — it exists because manual paralleling, judged only by watching two voltmeters and a frequency meter, has a long history of getting the phase-angle condition wrong under time pressure, with real equipment damage as the consequence; §3's three-condition rule is exactly what that panel is enforcing automatically.

10.lecture:
§1 · DOL and star-delta starting

Direct-on-line (DOL) starting connects the motor straight across full line voltage — simplest and cheapest, but drawing high inrush current, typically \(6\)–\(8\times\) rated full-load current, briefly at start, while delivering full starting torque. Star-delta starting temporarily connects windings normally run in delta into star (wye) for starting, then switches to delta once up to speed. Because star reduces both the line-current relationship (Lesson 6) and the voltage each winding actually sees, the two effects combine to cut inrush current to roughly a third of the DOL value. Starting torque, which scales with the square of applied voltage, falls by essentially the same proportion, to roughly a third — the reduced inrush is never free; it costs starting torque in direct proportion.

§2 · Overloads and protection classes

Thermal overload relays protect a motor from sustained overcurrent — not short-circuit, which is the breaker or fuse's job — by modeling winding heating and tripping before insulation damage occurs. They are sized to the motor's rated full-load current, and their trip class (Class 10, 20, 30, and so on) states how many seconds the relay allows at locked-rotor current before tripping: a Class 10 relay trips within roughly 10 seconds at locked-rotor current, a Class 30 up to about 30 seconds. The class must match the motor's actual thermal capability during a normal start — a mismatched class can either nuisance-trip on routine starts or fail to protect the motor during a genuine stall.

§3 · Variable-frequency drives — the torque curve and the bearings

A VFD changes both frequency and voltage supplied to the motor, shifting synchronous speed \( N_s=120f/p \) and therefore the entire torque-speed curve up or down the speed axis — the mechanism behind smooth, controllable speed rather than fixed-speed on/off control, and it allows a controlled, low-current start by ramping frequency up from near zero instead of DOL's inrush spike. What a VFD does that DOL never did: its high-frequency PWM switching can induce small stray voltages on the motor shaft itself, through capacitive coupling across the air gap. Left with no safe path to ground, those voltages discharge as tiny electrical arcs through the bearing's lubricant film — over time, this electrical-discharge-machining effect pits and flutes the bearing races, a failure mode that simply does not exist on a DOL-started motor with no VFD in the circuit.

§4 · Motor circuit anatomy — the working method

From the motor control center bucket: incoming isolator/disconnect, then short-circuit protection (fuse or breaker), then the contactor (the switching element), then the overload relay (thermal protection), then cable, then the motor terminal box. Reading this chain in order — and knowing what each stage protects against, short-circuit versus sustained overload versus simple switching — is the working method for troubleshooting why a motor circuit tripped, and which single component is the actual suspect.

Key trade-off — star-delta

Starting current and starting torque both fall to roughly 1/3 of DOL — a reduced-current start always costs proportional starting torque.

  • Wye/delta relationships (Lesson 6) — the basis of star-delta current reduction.
  • The torque-speed curve and slip (Lesson 8) — what a VFD shifts.

quiz.q:
Star-delta current and torque

Problem. A motor’s DOL starting current is \(7\times\) full-load current, with full DOL starting torque. Estimate the star-delta starting current (as a multiple of FLC) and starting torque (as a fraction of DOL torque).
quiz.solution: Solution. Star-delta current \(\approx7/3\approx2.33\times\) FLC. Star-delta torque \(\approx1/3\) of DOL torque — both cut by roughly the same factor of 3, so the reduced inrush is not free.

quiz.q:
Will the overload trip during a normal start?

Problem. A motor with locked-rotor current \(6\times\) FLC is fitted with a Class 30 overload relay. During a normal start it draws \(6\times\)FLC for about 8 seconds while accelerating. Will the relay likely trip, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. No. A Class 30 relay allows roughly 30 seconds at locked-rotor current before tripping, well beyond the 8-second normal start — it should ride through a routine start without nuisance-tripping, while still protecting against a genuine stall that persists past its rated time.

quiz.q:
Why VFDs pit bearing races

Problem. Explain why a motor driven by a VFD is more likely to develop pitted bearing races over time than the same motor started DOL, and name one mitigation.
quiz.solution: Solution. The VFD’s high-frequency PWM switching induces small stray voltages on the motor shaft through capacitive coupling; without a safe discharge path, these voltages repeatedly arc through the bearing’s lubricant film, pitting and fluting the races (electrical discharge machining). A shaft grounding ring, or an insulated bearing on the non-drive end, gives that stray voltage a safe path to ground instead of the bearing.

quiz.q: DOL starting connects the motor:
quiz.solution: DOL is the simplest, full-voltage starting method.

quiz.q: DOL starting current is typically:
quiz.solution: A brief, large inrush is characteristic of DOL starting.

quiz.q: Star-delta starting reduces both:
choices: Starting current AND starting torque, to roughly 1/3 of DOL values
Only starting current, with torque unaffected
Only starting torque, with current unaffected
quiz.solution: The reduction is proportional in both quantities, not free.

quiz.q: Thermal overload relays protect against:
choices: Sustained overcurrent, based on modeled winding heating
Loss of a single phase only
quiz.solution: Short-circuit protection is the separate job of a fuse or breaker.

quiz.q: An overload relay's trip class describes:
choices: How many seconds it allows at locked-rotor current before tripping
The number of poles on the motor
quiz.solution: Class 10/20/30 states the allowed time at locked-rotor current.

quiz.q: A VFD changes motor speed by:
choices: Varying supply frequency (and voltage), shifting the torque-speed curve
Physically changing the number of poles
quiz.solution: Frequency sets synchronous speed directly, moving the whole curve.

quiz.q: A VFD can cause bearing damage through:
choices: Stray shaft voltages discharging as small arcs through the bearing lubricant
quiz.solution: PWM switching induces shaft voltages that arc through bearings without mitigation.

quiz.q: A shaft grounding ring on a VFD-driven motor:
choices: Gives stray shaft voltage a safe path to ground, protecting the bearings
Replaces the need for an overload relay
quiz.solution: It diverts the stray voltage away from the bearing's lubricant film.

10.kuwait:
A plant switching an older DOL-started fan or pump motor over to VFD control for energy savings, without also adding shaft grounding or insulated bearings, is a common, avoidable setup for exactly the §3 bearing-pitting failure mode — a failure that can show up as premature bearing replacement months or a couple of years into VFD service, misdiagnosed as a bad bearing batch rather than the actual root cause.

Reading an MCC bucket's fault history against §4's chain — isolator, short-circuit device, contactor, overload, cable, terminal box — is standard first-response troubleshooting: a tripped overload with no short-circuit device operation points toward a sustained mechanical overload on the driven equipment, while a tripped breaker points toward a genuine fault, and starting the investigation at the wrong end wastes real time during a plant outage.

11.lecture:
§1 · Shock and arc-flash hazards, respected properly

Electric shock severity depends on the current actually driven through the body, not on voltage alone — even a modest voltage can drive dangerous current through wet skin or direct contact, while the same voltage may be harmless through dry, unbroken skin. This is why "low voltage" control circuits are never treated as automatically safe. Arc-flash is a distinct hazard entirely: a short-circuit fault in a high-energy system can release an explosive arc — intense heat, a pressure wave, molten metal spray — severe enough to injure someone standing near an energized panel without any direct contact at all, purely from proximity. Arc-flash risk assessment and the correct PPE are sized to the specific equipment and available fault current at that point, not a one-size-fits-all rule, and are a separate requirement from ordinary shock protection.

§2 · Lockout-tagout, as a mechanical engineer practices it

Lockout-tagout means physically isolating a source of hazardous energy — electrical, but equally stored mechanical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy — and applying a personal lock and tag before work begins, verified by an actual test for zero energy rather than trust in a switch position or a label. A mechanical engineer maintaining a pump or gearbox routinely locks out the electrical supply to that equipment as a standard first step, even though the hands-on work that follows is entirely mechanical.

§3 · Insulation resistance and clamp-meter measurements

Insulation resistance testing (a megohmmeter or "megger" test) applies a DC test voltage across a winding's insulation to ground and reads the resulting leakage current as a resistance, typically in megohms. A single reading is of limited use alone — what counts as "good" varies with motor size, insulation class, age, and ambient conditions. The real diagnostic value is in the trend: a winding whose measured value falls steadily over successive tests, even while each individual reading still technically clears a generic threshold, is showing progressive degradation and heading toward failure, while a value stable near its historical baseline is healthy regardless of the absolute number. This is exactly why IR is logged and trended over a machine's life, not treated as a simple spot-check. Clamp-meter current measurements, taken without breaking the circuit, are the practical field tool for checking phase balance (Lesson 6), confirming actual running current against nameplate full-load current, and catching an abnormal draw before it causes a trip.

§4 · The boundary line between mechanical and electrical scope

The practical, widely-used convention: a mechanical scope of work ends at the motor's terminal box — the point where field wiring lands on the motor's own terminals. Everything from there back toward the supply — cable, MCC, protection devices — is electrical scope, requiring an electrically qualified and authorized person; the motor frame, coupling, bearings, and driven equipment remain squarely mechanical scope. This boundary exists because working inside a terminal box, even just to check a connection, means working on live-capable electrical equipment, which requires specific electrical safety qualification and LOTO authority that a mechanical scope of work does not automatically confer. Crossing that line without the right authorization is both a safety violation and, in most well-run plants, a policy violation — regardless of how simple the task looks.

Key habit — read the trend, not the number

Insulation resistance is diagnostic as a trend over repeated tests, not as a single pass/fail reading.

quiz.q:
Reading an IR trend

Problem. A motor’s insulation resistance is measured at commissioning (500 MΩ), then at 3 years (200 MΩ), 5 years (80 MΩ), and 7 years (20 MΩ). Every reading still exceeds a generic 1 MΩ "minimum acceptable" threshold for this motor class. Is the insulation actually healthy? Explain using the trend, not the individual readings.
quiz.solution: Solution. No. Despite every reading technically clearing the generic 1 MΩ threshold, the value has fallen by roughly a factor of 25 over 7 years — a clear, steady degradation trend. The trend, not any single passing number, signals this winding is deteriorating toward failure; waiting for it to finally drop below the generic threshold means waiting until failure is close rather than catching the problem while there is time to plan a repair.

quiz.q:
Whose scope is the terminal box?

Problem. A mechanical technician is asked to "just check the connections" inside a running motor’s terminal box, since a suspected loose connection might be causing vibration. Is this within the mechanical scope of work? Explain the boundary.
quiz.solution: Solution. No. The terminal box is inside the electrical scope boundary; opening it, especially on live or live-capable equipment, requires electrical qualification and proper lockout/authorization, not general maintenance access. Mechanical scope covers the frame, coupling, and driven equipment; anything at or behind the terminal connections is electrical scope — the correct action is to request an electrically qualified person, not open the box directly.

quiz.q:
Shock vs. arc-flash

Problem. Explain the difference between shock hazard and arc-flash hazard, and why standing near, but not touching, an energized panel during a fault can still cause serious injury.
quiz.solution: Solution. Shock hazard requires actual current flow through a person’s body via direct or near contact with an energized conductor. Arc-flash is distinct: a fault can release explosive energy — heat, pressure, molten metal — that injures anyone nearby purely through proximity, with no contact required at all. This is why arc-flash PPE and boundary distances are assessed separately from, and in addition to, ordinary shock protection.

quiz.q: Electric shock severity depends primarily on:
choices: Current through the body, not voltage alone
The color of the conductor's insulation
The time of day the contact occurs
quiz.solution: Current, not voltage in isolation, is what actually harms the body.

quiz.q: Arc-flash hazard:
choices: Can injure someone near an energized panel without any direct contact
Only occurs with direct skin contact
quiz.solution: Proximity alone is enough for arc-flash injury during a fault.

quiz.q: Lockout-tagout requires:
choices: Physically isolating hazardous energy and verifying zero energy before work begins
Simply switching off a breaker and starting work
Only applies to electrical energy, never mechanical
A verbal confirmation from a supervisor only
quiz.solution: Verification of zero energy, not just a switch position, is the actual requirement.

quiz.q: A single insulation resistance (IR) reading:
choices: Is of limited diagnostic value alone; the trend over time matters more
Is always sufficient on its own to judge motor health
Is meaningless and should never be recorded
quiz.solution: Trend over repeated tests is the real diagnostic signal.

quiz.q: A steadily falling IR trend, even with each reading still technically passing:
choices: Only matters if a reading actually fails
quiz.solution: The direction of change is the signal, not any single number's pass/fail status.

quiz.q: Clamp-meter current measurements are useful for:
choices: Checking phase balance and comparing running current to nameplate FLC
quiz.solution: A clamp meter reads current without breaking the circuit — ideal for routine field checks.

quiz.q: The conventional boundary between mechanical and electrical work scope is:
quiz.solution: Terminal connections mark where mechanical scope ends and electrical scope begins.

quiz.q: Opening a motor terminal box requires:
choices: Electrical qualification and proper authorization, not general mechanical access
No special authorization if the motor looks off
Only a flashlight and a screwdriver
Permission from the equipment vendor only
quiz.solution: Terminal-box access is electrical scope regardless of how simple the check appears.

11.kuwait:
A plant's predictive-maintenance program logging insulation resistance on every large motor at each scheduled outage, rather than only when a motor has already shown a problem, is applying §3 exactly as intended: the trend across several outages, not any single outage's reading, is what schedules a rewind or replacement before an in-service failure takes the equipment down unexpectedly, often at the worst possible time.

A near-miss log entry describing a mechanical technician who was stopped before opening a motor terminal box to "just check a connection" is a live example of §4's boundary holding — the honest, useful outcome of a good safety culture is not zero incidents reported, but near-misses caught and corrected before they become the real thing.
